coredata.dc:title: Cellulose nanocomposite hydrogel-conductive, antimicrobial and high tenacity sensor for information encryption, human motion monitoring and animal behavior studies

coredata.dc:description: Conductive hydrogels have garnered significant attention in advanced applications, particularly in wearable strain sensors. Nevertheless, the challenge of fabricating wearable sensors that demonstrate both optimal mechanical properties and high conductivity persists. In this study, we successfully developed a multifunctional hydrogel sensor comprising acrylamide (AM), polyethylene glycol (PEG), and sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC). The resulting hydrogel demonstrated favorable mechanical properties, electrical conductivity, adhesion, antimicrobial activity, antifreezing capability, moisture retention, fluorescence, and strain sensitivity. Notably, the sensor retains its flexibility and electrical conductivity even at −20 °C. The incorporation of doped carbon quantum dots imparts pronounced fluorescence properties to the hydrogel, facilitating clear topographical imaging and paving the way for potential applications in information encryption. The fabricated strain sensors exhibit broad applicability and high reliability in human motion monitoring, allowing for secure attachment to the human body without the need for additional adhesives. Both large movements, such as joint bending (e.g., wrists and knees), and subtle movements, such as chewing and swallowing, can be accurately monitored. Furthermore, this study aims to integrate flexible hydrogel sensor technology with pet behavior monitoring, with the objective of identifying the emotional states of dogs. The ultimate goal is to establish a novel model for animal behavior research.

head.ce:title: Cellulose nanocomposite hydrogel-conductive, antimicrobial and high tenacity sensor for information encryption, human motion monitoring and animal behavior studies

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Conductive hydrogels have garnered significant attention in advanced applications, particularly in wearable strain sensors. Nevertheless, the challenge of fabricating wearable sensors that demonstrate both optimal mechanical properties and high conductivity persists. In this study, we successfully developed a multifunctional hydrogel sensor comprising acrylamide (AM), polyethylene glycol (PEG), and sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC). The resulting hydrogel demonstrated favorable mechanical properties, electrical conductivity, adhesion, antimicrobial activity, antifreezing capability, moisture retention, fluorescence, and strain sensitivity. Notably, the sensor retains its flexibility and electrical conductivity even at −20 °C. The incorporation of doped carbon quantum dots imparts pronounced fluorescence properties to the hydrogel, facilitating clear topographical imaging and paving the way for potential applications in information encryption. The fabricated strain sensors exhibit broad applicability and high reliability in human motion monitoring, allowing for secure attachment to the human body without the need for additional adhesives. Both large movements, such as joint bending (e.g., wrists and knees), and subtle movements, such as chewing and swallowing, can be accurately monitored. Furthermore, this study aims to integrate flexible hydrogel sensor technology with pet behavior monitoring, with the objective of identifying the emotional states of dogs. The ultimate goal is to establish a novel model for animal behavior research.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors confirm that the data supporting the findings of this study are available within the article and its supplementary materials.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, research on functional hydrogels has become a focal point, which has emerged as pivotal components in various fields, including medical detection [1–3], solar steam generators [4,5], green antifouling coatings [6], human-computer interaction [7–9], adhesives [10–13], energy storage materials [14,15], wearable strain sensors [16–18] and other domains. Among these, wearable strain sensors are crucial for the continuous and direct observation of physical conditions such as muscle movement, joint flexibility, and the early detection of disease symptoms. The practical applications of wearable strain sensors depend on several key properties, including high flexibility, mechanical stability, biocompatibility, and electrical conductivity [19–21]. To address this demand, conventional materials such as metals and rigid polymers are often combined with elastomers to construct the sensors. However, conventional materials exhibit limited ductility and inherent stiffness when integrated into wearable devices, potentially causing discomfort to users. Therefore, there is a pressing need to design new sensor materials that exhibit conductive properties, flexibility, and biocompatibility to meet the requirements of practical applications and working scenarios.

Polyacrylamide (PAM) hydrogels, distinguished by their high water content, exceptional mechanical properties, and flexibility, have emerged as optimal materials for wearable strain sensors [22,23]. To enhance the sensing performance of these hydrogels, the incorporation of ionic liquids or metal nanoparticles is frequently utilized [24–26]. However, achieving a balance between electrical conductivity and mechanical strength remains a challenge, as ion doping tends to reduce the hydrogel's strength. Furthermore, the high water content of hydrogels makes them susceptible to freezing at low temperatures, and water loss can further degrade the performance of strain sensors. In addressing the challenges associated with polyacrylamide hydrogels, Yu et al. proposed a preparation strategy involving polyacrylamide and agar, with MXenes acting as a catalyst to accelerate free radical polymerization. This method ensures stable functionality even at very low temperatures of −30 °C (with a stable response range of 250 %, linearity of 0.995, and a gauge factor of 1.25) [27]. Wang et al. reported the construction of a chemically crosslinked poly(acrylamide-co-polyacrylic acid) network through Fe3+-mediated physical crosslinking [28]. The resulting hydrogels, featuring a dual crosslinked network structure, exhibited an ultra-high tensile strength of 42 MPa in the longitudinal direction, while lignocellulosic hydrogels with intrinsic electrical conductivity demonstrated a wide range of sensing capabilities for strain, temperature, and light. Incorporating silver-loaded polydopamine nanoparticles (Ag@PDA) into thermally crosslinked methacrylamide chitosan (CSMA) and acrylamide networks allowed Wu et al. to construct a multifunctional hydrogel sensor exhibiting a sensitivity of 2.13 within a strain range of 65 % to 150 % [29]. The sensor demonstrated satisfactory biocompatibility, alongside excellent antioxidant and antimicrobial activities. Song et al. discovered that phytic acid (PA) interacts with various polymers (polyacrylamide and polyacrylic acid) in markedly different manners, exhibiting both plasticizing effects and phase separation [30]. This unique mechanism enabled the hydrogel flexible pressure sensor to achieve high sensitivity (9.00 kPa−1, <15 kPa) and an extensive sensing range (from 3.7 Pa to 1.2 MPa) simultaneously. Furthermore, the sensor exhibited superior performance in upper-level low-pressure sensing, successfully detecting subtle pressures caused by acoustic waves and air currents, as well as moderate pressures from speech and finger touch, and high amplitudes of foot pressure. While exploring self-adhesive hydrogel sensors, Zhang et al. identified that sodium lignosulfonate significantly enhances the self-adhesion of hydrogels, attributed to the presence of catechol and semiquinone groups in oxidized lignin [31]. These semiquinone/catechol groups are capable of forming hydrogen bonds, coordination bonds, covalent bonds, and π-π interactions with various species within the hydrogel system, which results in robust adhesion to both hydrophobic and hydrophilic surfaces. In their investigation of underwater adhesive hydrogel sensors, Li et al. achieved underwater adhesion by enabling the hydrogel to rapidly absorb the water layer between the hydrogel and the substrate via hydrophilic nanoparticles. This process is complemented by the synergistic repulsion of the water molecule layer through a hydrophobic component, thereby ensuring van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonding interactions at the binding surface [32]. Unfortunately, most research reports primarily enumerate the related properties and potential utilization of modified hydrogel sensors, such as the enhancement of mechanical properties, adhesion, frost resistance, and electrical conductivity. There is a notable lack of research focused on functional properties and practical applications.

This study developed a hydrogel using a one-pot method with a glycerol-water solvent (volume ratio of glycerol to water: 4:6), which significantly improved its moisturization and anti-freezing properties. The incorporation of polyethylene glycol (PEG) enhanced the traditional polyacrylamide (PAM) hydrogel through non-covalent cross-linking, achieving a tensile strength of 0.38 MPa and an elongation at break of 1432 %. Ionic liquids and Ag-PDA@SiO2 nanoparticles were added to enhance electrical conductivity, while silver conferred antimicrobial properties. Additionally, carbon quantum dots enabled UV-induced visualization for potential information encryption applications. The hydrogel's sensitive resistance changes allow it to detect subtle movements when worn on the human body. We pioneered its application in animal behavior monitoring by attaching it to a dog's tail to capture signals related to emotional states. The glycerol-enhanced hydrogel ensures long-term durability and moisturization, making it suitable for green smart wearables. This approach offers non-invasive, sustainable solutions for understanding animal emotions and behavior, thereby advancing research in wearable technology and behavioral science.

Acrylamide (AM, 99 %), N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA, 99 %) and Dopamine hydrochloride (DOPA) were purchased from Anneji (Shanghai) Pharmaceutical Chemical Co., Ltd. Sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC, Mw = 700,000 and DS = 0.7) and silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.8 %) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Silica treated by KH550 (20 nm) was purchased from Shanghai MCC New Material Co., Ltd. 1-Hexyl-3-methylimidazolium ([HMIm]BF4) was purchased from Cool chemical science and technology (Beijing) Co., Ltd. Polyethylene Glycol (PEG, Mw = 12,000) was purchased from Beijing Jintai Hongda Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Ammonium persulfate (APS, 98 %) was purchased from Anhui Zesheng Technology Co., Ltd. Citric acid monohydrate (99.5 %) was purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. Urea (99 %) was purchased from Beijing Beihua Fine Chemicals Co., Ltd. Tris (99.5 %) was purchased from Lanjieke Technologya Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used without further purification.

Initially, 0.1 g of SiO2 and 0.1 g of DOPA were sequentially dissolved in Tris buffer (20 mL, pH 7.5), and the mixture was stirred for 12 h. At this point the solution changes from colorless to light black. The resulting SiO2-modified product was recovered. Following this, 0.04 g of AgNO3 was added to the solution, and the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 24 h to produce a dark brown solution. This solution was then sonicated for 30 min and subsequently subjected to high-speed centrifugation (12,000 rpm for 20 min). The upper layer was discarded, and the product was lyophilized for 12 h to yield light brown powdered Ag-PDA@SiO2 nanoparticles, which were sealed and stored at 4 °C for future use.

The synthesis of carbon quantum dots was achieved using a hydrothermal method. Specifically, 0.13 g of anhydrous citric acid and 0.11 g of urea were dissolved in 3 mL of deionised water. The resulting mixture was stirred for 10 min at room temperature. Then, the mixture was transferred to an autoclave reactor and reacted at 160 °C for 8 h, yielding a dark brown solution. This solution was then subjected to centrifugation at 10,000 rpm, followed by dialysis using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of 500 Da for 12 h. The dark brown carbon quantum dot powder was obtained by freeze-drying at −20 °C, after which it was sealed and stored at 4 °C in conditions that prevented exposure to light.

The hydrogel was prepared using a straightforward one-pot method. The procedure commenced with the mixing of 4 mL of glycerol and 6 mL of water to serve as the solvent. Subsequently, 3 g of AM and 0.1 g of PEG were added to this mixture and stirred thoroughly. Following this, 1 mL of [HMIm]BF4 (1.49 g/mL) and 0.01 g of Ag-PDA@SiO2 nanoparticles were introduced into the system and uniformly dispersed with 0.1 g of CMC. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 10 min, after which 0.004 g of MBA, a cross-linking agent, and 0.04 g of APS, an initiator, were incorporated. The mixture was then stirred at high speed for 10 min, followed by slow stirring for 5 min to allow the bubbles generated during stirring to dissipate. The resultant mixture was poured into a mold and placed in an oven at 60 °C for 4 h to obtain hydrogels of various shapes. Finally, CDs powder was formulated into a fluorescent liquid at a concentration of 1 mg/mL, and different patterns were topographed on the hydrogel using the mold. To investigate the effect of PEG on the mechanical properties of the hydrogels, a series of hydrogels with varying PEG concentrations (0 wt%, 0.5 wt%, 1.0 wt%, 1.5 wt%, 2.0 wt%) were prepared. Additionally, a series of hydrogels with different concentrations of Ag-PDA@SiO2 nanoparticles (0.1 wt%, 0.2 wt%, 0.3 wt%, 0.4 wt%) were developed to assess the impact of nanoparticles on the antimicrobial properties of the hydrogels. Glycerol-free hydrogels were also prepared to serve as a control group for antifreeze and moisturizing experiments. Furthermore, a series of hydrogels with varying compositions were prepared for characterization, including XRD analysis, to observe the influence of different substances on the reticular structure of the hydrogels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of chemical and morphological structure of hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The infrared absorption spectra of the completely dried hydrogels were measured in transmission mode using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, TENSOR II FTIR, Germany) over a wavelength range of 4000 to 400 cm−1. The internal crystal structure was determined using an X-ray crystal diffractometer (XRD, D/MAX-2500). Specifically, the hydrogels were cut into pieces, dried, and then scanned in the 2θ range of 10o to 70o to obtain the X-ray diffraction patterns of the different components. The rheological properties of the hydrogels were examined by testing the energy storage modulus (G′) and loss modulus (G″) using a DHR-1 rheometer (TA Instruments). Time scans were performed at 1 % shear strain, 10 Hz, and 25 °C, while frequency scans were conducted at 0.5 % shear strain, over a range of 1 to 100 rad/s, at 25 °C. For fluorescence properties test, the hydrogels were sliced into thin sections. PL spectra at different excitation wavelengths and fluorescence spectra of varying carbon quantum dot concentrations were measured on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-2700). The freezing temperature of hydrogels was characterized by a differential scanning calorimeter (DSC 8000, PE, Germany) from 5 to −70 °C at a cooling rate of 10 °C·min−1. Furthermore, the micromorphology of the synthesised carbon quantum dots was observed and photographed with a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Hitachi, H-7650). The morphology of the hydrogels was observed and photographed using a field-emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) (SM-7500F, JEOL, Japan). The samples were dissolved, freeze-dried, and coated with a thin layer of metal for measurement.

The mechanical properties of the samples were evaluated using a standard tensile testing machine, with tensile tests conducted at a constant speed of 100 mm/min. The samples used for these tensile tests were cylindrical (cross-sectional area × length: 9.6 mm2 × 9 mm). Additionally, the compression properties of cylindrical samples, each with a diameter of 15 mm and a height of 15 mm, were assessed through compression tests performed at the same constant speed of 100 mm/min. The mean outcomes from three tests were calculated to ensure accuracy. Tensile stress (σ) was defined as the applied load divided by the original cross-sectional area of the sample, while tensile strain (ε) was determined as the change in length divided by the original length. The toughness of the hydrogels was calculated by integrating the area under the stress-strain curve for each specimen. Young's modulus was determined as the slope of the linear region of the tensile stress-strain curve within the strain range of 0–5 %. The dissipated energy is calculated from the area of the closed graph enclosed by the stress-strain curve. The tensile stress (σ) and tensile strain (ε) were calculated by the following equations:

In the present study, Ag-PDA@SiO2 nanoparticles with varying mass fractions (0.1 wt%, 0.2 wt%, 0.3 wt%, and 0.4 wt%) were incorporated into a hydrogel to prepare cylindrical samples with a diameter of 10 mm. These samples were placed under irradiation with UV light for 12 h, after which the samples were placed on media containing E. coli or S. aureus. Following an additional incubation period of 14 h at 37 °C, the size of the resulting zone of inhibition was measured.

The adhesive property test of the hydrogels was evaluated using a standard tensile testing machine, with tensile tests conducted at a constant speed of 50 mm/min. The samples used for this test were cut into slices (cross-sectional area × thickness: 100 mm2 × 2 mm).

For the anti-freezing and moisturizing tests, hydrogels were prepared as a comparison group using pure water as the solvent. In the antifreeze test, samples of both hydrogels were stored at −20 °C for 24 h, after which their flexibility and electrical conductivity were examined.

For the moisture retention test, cylindrical hydrogels of identical dimensions (15 mm in diameter and 15 mm in height) were maintained at a constant temperature of 25 °C for 7 days. The weight of the samples and their dimensions were recorded at various storage intervals. The weight loss was calculated according to the following equation:

The PAM/CMC/PEG/Ag-PDA@SiO2 hydrogel was fabricated into a cylindrical shape (cross-sectional area × length: 9.6 mm2 × 8 mm). Wires were connected to the hydrogel at intervals of 20 mm, with the other ends linked to a digital bridge (TH2830). The hydrogel was secured in place using a standard tensile testing machine. Utilizing the tensile test settings of the universal testing machine, various tensile strain programs were configured on the computer to measure the relative resistance changes of the hydrogel during different stretching and recovery processes. Relative resistance was calculated according to the following equation:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Strain-gel sensor testing and motion monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication of a PAM/CMC/PEG/Ag-PDA@SiO2 hydrogel sheet (length × width × thickness: 40 mm × 10 mm × 3 mm) was performed, with wires connected to the extremities of the hydrogel. The sensors were affixed to various joints (fingers, wrists, neck, etc.) of the experimenter and the tail of a dog. The increase and recovery of electrical signals resulting from the flexion and extension of the adhered parts were monitored, enabling the detection of movement through the analysis of these electrical signals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and characterization of conductive hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrogels were prepared using a one-pot method, as illustrated in Fig. 1a. A mixture of glycerol and water served as the solvent, into which AM, PEG, [HMIm]BF4, and Ag-PDA@SiO2 were sequentially introduced and homogeneously dispersed with CMC. MBA was utilized as the cross-linking agent, while APS functioned as the initiator, triggering free-radical polymerization to form the hydrogel at 60 °C [33]. The process for synthesizing Ag-PDA@SiO2 nanoparticles entailed the modification of silver nanoparticles on the exterior of hydrated SiO2 using an in situ chemical reduction technique [34]. In this procedure, PDA was deposited onto hydrated SiO2 by means of the oxidative polymerization of dopamine in a Tris-buffer solution (pH = 7.5). During this process, dopamine self-polymerizes and adheres to the surface of hydrated SiO2. Following this, the amino and hydroxyl groups present in PDA engage with AgNO3, leading to the in situ reduction of the salt to the Ag0 state through the action of phenolic hydroxyl groups, which ultimately results in the anchoring of silver nanoparticles to the PDA-coated hydrated SiO2. A schematic representation of the nanoparticle fabrication process is presented in Fig. 1b. The interaction mechanism within the hydrogel network is depicted in Fig. 1c. Hydrogen bonds were established between PEG and PAM, as well as between the nanoparticles and both PEG and PAM. The PEG and PAM network facilitated non-covalent dynamic cross-linking interactions, leading to a more aligned chain arrangement and the formation of a composite reticulation [35]. The molecular chains of CMC physically entangle other chains, which could enhance the mechanical performances of the hydrogels [36,37]. The Ag-PDA@SiO2 nanoparticles enhanced hydrogen bonding and cross-linking sites, allowing for improved dissipation of mechanical forces within the hydrogels [38]. As demonstrated in Fig. 1d, the hydrogels in this study were enhanced through the incorporation of PEG, resulting in superior mechanical properties compared to conventional hydrogels. The sensing capabilities of these hydrogels were markedly improved by the inclusion of ionic liquids and nanoparticles, with the addition of silver nanoparticles imparting antimicrobial properties [39–41]. Utilizing a glycerol-water mixture as a solvent bestows the hydrogels with anti-freezing and moisturizing characteristics, enabling them to endure extreme environmental conditions. Furthermore, the integration of carbon quantum dots into the hydrogels facilitates the emission of fluorescent light, a feature that holds promise for applications in information encryption. Consequently, this multifunctional hydrogel is poised to significantly broaden the scope of future applications (Fig. 1e).

The evolution of microstructures and chemical interactions typically results in specific changes in material properties [42]. As depicted in Fig. 2a, the fracture surface of the PAM/CMC hydrogel displayed uniform circular holes, in contrast to the smooth lamellar structure of the PAM hydrogel. Furthermore, the PAM/CMC/PEG/Ag-PDA@SiO2 exhibited a dense network with a mountainous structure, indicating that a significant amount of energy is expended during the fracture process. These compelling results align with the mechanical property outcomes presented in Fig. 2. Furthermore, the EDS mapping images (Fig. 2b) illustrated that C, O, N elements were uniformly dispersed in the hydrogel.

As demonstrated in Fig. 2c, the characteristic peaks at 3338 cm−1 and 3165 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of NH bonds. The peaks at 1650 cm−1 and 1602 cm−1 correspond to the CO and CC stretching vibrations of acrylamide (AM) [43–45], respectively. With the introduction of CMC and PEG, the NH stretching vibration of the composite hydrogel shifted from 3338 cm−1 to 3355 cm−1, indicating the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds between PAM and CMC, as well as between PAM and PEG [46]. Similarly, the PAM/CMC/PEG hydrogels exhibited broad and strong peaks in the range of 3154–3400 cm−1, attributed to the intermolecular hydrogen bonding among PAM, CMC, and PEG. The peak at 2922 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of numerous methylene groups in the PAM chains, while the peaks at 1258 cm−1 and 1031 cm−1 are attributed to the asymmetric stretching vibration of C-O-C and ether bonds in the CMC chain [47]. In the presence of Ag+ ions, ionic coordination was introduced within the PAM/PEG molecular chain, resulting in an additional peak at 1101 cm−1 in the IR spectrum.

In addition, the interactions between the gel components were examined by using XRD analysis (Fig. 2d). It was observed that the intensity of the PAM peak increased with the addition of CMC. This phenomenon can be attributed to the introduction of CMC, which led to a more uniform dispersion of the PAM chains. Consequently, this facilitated the formation of hydrogen bonds between the PAM chains. As a result, there was an increase in crystallinity, leading to a more complete and ordered crystal structure, as well as an enhancement of diffraction effects. In contrast, the addition of PEG resulted in a decrease in the intensity of the PAM peak, which can be attributed to its interference with the formation of hydrogen bonds between the PAM chains. This phenomenon may be explained by the interpenetration of PEG chains in PAM networks. PEG contains numerous hydrophilic groups (e.g., −OH) on its surface. The hydrogen bonding between water molecules and these hydrophilic groups, along with the amide group of acrylamide, contributes to enhanced mechanical properties [48]. The PAM/CMC/PEG/Ag-PDA@SiO2 hydrogel exhibited silver (111) and (200) diffraction peaks at 38o and 44o, respectively, indicating the successful incorporation of silver nanoparticles.

The rheological properties of the composite hydrogels were further evaluated through rheological tests. The internal structure of the hydrogels was characterized by the energy storage modulus (G′) and the loss modulus (G″). As illustrated in Fig. 2e, at 25 °C, a frequency of 10 Hz, and an applied strain of 1 %, the storage modulus (G′) consistently exceeded the loss modulus (G″). The curves for G′ and G″ exhibited a smooth trend, indicating that the hydrogels maintained stable elastic properties under constant conditions [49]. Furthermore, dynamic frequency scanning measurements (Fig. 2f) revealed that the incorporation of PEG into the PAM/CMC/PEG/Ag-PDA@SiO2 hydrogel enhanced the G′ value compared to that of the PAM/CMC/Ag-PDA@SiO2 hydrogel alone. This observation further supports the hypothesis that PEG can act as a toughening agent.

As demonstrated in Fig. 3a, the PAM/CMC/PEG/Ag-PDA@SiO2 hydrogels exhibited a load-bearing capacity of 200 g and 350 g, along with a stretchability exceeding six times their original length without any evident damage. Furthermore, the hydrogels displayed exceptional puncture resistance, as evidenced by their ability to withstand sharp knife blades without fracturing, thereby signifying their superior mechanical strength, ductility, and load-bearing capacity. The hydrogel also demonstrates notable resilience, exhibiting the capacity for autonomous recovery following substantial mechanical compression. The regulatory impact of PEG on the mechanical properties of PAM hydrogels was further investigated, as illustrated in Fig. 3b. With the augmentation of PEG addition from 0.0 wt% to 1.5 wt%, the strain at break increased from 1022 % to 1432 %, while the tensile strength enhanced from 0.2 MPa to 0.38 MPa. The Young's modulus decreased from 0.057 MPa to 0.055 MPa, and the toughness increased from 1.18 kJ/m3 to 2.37 kJ/m3 (Fig. 3d). These findings suggest that PEG enhances the toughness of the hydrogel, thereby improving its mechanical properties. The incorporation of polyethylene glycol (PEG) results in a denser crosslinked network within the hydrogel. The formation of non-covalent dynamic crosslinking interactions between the PEG and polyacrylamide (PAM) networks leads to a composite mesh structure. Furthermore, PEG is capable of forming ionic and hydrogen bonds with Ag-PDA@SiO2 nanoparticles. The increased number of hydrogen bonding and cross-linking sites enhances the hydrogel's ability to dissipate mechanical forces, allowing the material to endure greater energy without fracturing under stress. Conversely, the integration of PEG chains within the PAM chains facilitates the release of internal stresses present in the original PAM chains. The crosslinked network is organized in a more orderly fashion, improving the sliding capability between chains, which results in a decrease in Young's modulus and an increase in toughness. However, increasing the PEG concentration from 1.5 wt% to 2.0 wt% led to a reduction in strain at break, from 1432 % to 1311 %, and a decline in tensile strength from 0.38 MPa to 0.26 MPa. Consequently, Young's modulus and toughness experienced a significant decrease, from 0.05495 MPa and 2.37121 kJ/m3 to 0.05013 MPa and 1.73458 kJ/m3, respectively. This phenomenon is primarily attributed to the increased cross-linking density within the hydrogel network, which restricts the available free volume and limits the mobility of polymer chains. The reduced molecular mobility diminishes the material's energy dissipation capacity, making it more vulnerable to fragmentation under external stress. Moreover, the introduction of excessive PEG content leads to a non-homogeneous distribution of components within the hydrogel matrix, resulting in localized agglomerations that undermine the mechanical integrity of the material. Consequently, this results in a significant reduction in both the Young's modulus and fracture toughness of the hydrogel network. Given the hydrogel's demonstrated capacity for exceptional mechanical properties, 1.5 % PEG addition was selected for further investigation.

In order to investigate the low hysteresis of hydrogels, cyclic tensile experiments (100 %, 300 %, 500 %, 700 %, 900 %) were performed, as demonstrated in Fig. 3e. It was observed that the energy dissipated by the hydrogels increased with the maximum strain. This phenomenon can be attributed to the strain process, which disrupts dynamic interactions such as ionic and hydrogen bonding within the hydrogel. Notably, even at the maximum strain of 900 %, the hydrogel exhibits no significant hysteresis in the cyclic curves, indicating its ultra-low hysteresis. Furthermore, 15 consecutive cyclic loading-unloading tests were performed at a strain of 160 % (Fig. 3f and g). The results showed that the maximum stress did not decrease significantly with cycling, and the dissipated energy stabilized. This suggests that the incorporation of PEG into the hydrogel enhances cross-linking and introduces a significant number of dynamically reversible hydrogen bonding sites. When the hydrogel is stretched, the dynamic interactions are temporarily disrupted. As ions and molecules migrate, the recovery of the hydrogel structure and the formation of hydrogen bonds are facilitated. These dynamic and reversible bonds can be reformed, resulting in a high level of fatigue resistance.

The compressive properties of hydrogels are equally crucial, prompting further investigation into the modulation of their mechanical properties under compression with varying concentrations of PEG. As illustrated in Fig. 3h, the compressive strength increased from 0.056 MPa to 0.102 MPa with the addition of PEG, ranging from 0.0 wt% to 1.5 wt% at a compressive strain of 40 %. Conversely, as illustrated in Fig. 3i and j, the compressive strength decreases from 0.102 MPa to 0.089 MPa when the PEG concentration increases from 1.5 wt% to 2.0 wt%. This reduction is attributed to the excessive amount of PEG leading to non-uniform dispersion. Additionally, the results obtained during tensile testing were validated through ten consecutive cyclic loading-unloading tests conducted at a compressive strain of 65 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of antimicrobial, adhesive, antifreeze and moisturizing properties
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogels possess abundant porous structures that facilitate bacterial reproduction. Their direct contact with human skin, particularly when used as wearable sensors for monitoring health and movement status, necessitates measures to prevent bacterial infection. As demonstrated in Fig. 4a, this study examined the bacteriostatic effects of the PAM/CMC/PEG/Ag-PDA@SiO2 composite hydrogel on E. coli and S. aureus. The findings revealed that the hydrogel exhibited a significant bacteriostatic effect on E. coli and S. aureus following the incorporation of Ag-PDA@SiO2 nanoparticles. Notably, the magnitude of this effect increased with the amount of Ag-PDA@SiO2 nanoparticles added, ranging from 0.1 wt% to 0.4 wt%. Consequently, the inhibitory effect was enhanced, and the width of the inhibition zone expanded from 3 mm to 4 mm. This enhancement is primarily attributed to the release of silver ions from Ag-PDA@SiO2 into the environment, which interact electrostatically with the negatively charged cell membranes. This interaction affects the permeability of the cell wall, leading to intracellular osmotic imbalance and cytoplasmic leakage, ultimately resulting in bacterial cell death. In subsequent studies, the amount of nanoparticles added was fixed at 0.1 wt%.

The ability of conductive hydrogels to adhere firmly to various substrates is crucial for the precise identification of minute deformations. The composite hydrogel exhibits a significant level of adhesion to surfaces of different shapes and materials, including plastic, wood, metal, glass, and rubber, indicating a broad compatibility with substrates (Fig. 4b). Importantly, the hydrogel demonstrates a high degree of adaptability to human skin, with no residue remaining post-removal. As shown in Fig. 4c, the strong adhesion of the hydrogel to various substrates is due to the plentiful hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups found on its surface. These functional groups promote the formation of hydrogen bonds and ionic dipole interactions with a broad range of materials, thus guaranteeing excellent adhesion characteristics [31,50]. To demonstrate the adhesion properties of hydrogels more specifically, we examined the adhesion of hydrogels to tissue samples after applying varying durations of compression (Fig. 4d). The results indicate that as the compression duration increased from 1 to 10 min, the adhesion performance improved from 35 to 50 kPa. This enhancement can be attributed to prolonged compression, which facilitates greater interaction between the molecules in the hydrogel and the cardboard surfaces, thereby forming stronger intermolecular bonds, including Van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonds.

In certain instances, the utilization of hydrogel sensors is essential in extreme environments. As illustrated in Fig. 5a, to investigate the anti-freezing and moisturizing capabilities of composite hydrogels, hydrogels composed solely of water as a solvent were prepared as a control group. These hydrogels were designated as group W, while those containing glycerol were referred to as group G. At 25 °C, no significant differences were observed between groups W and G. However, it was noted that the hydrogels without added glycerol froze into ice at −20 °C, resulting in visible fracture marks after stretching. In contrast, the hydrogels incorporating glycerol displayed no damage after being twisted and stretched, even after cooling to −20 °C, thereby demonstrating their remarkable freezing resistance under extreme temperature conditions. Furthermore, the hydrogels maintained stable electrical conductivity at sub-zero temperatures, as evidenced by the illumination of LED bulbs when the frozen hydrogels were utilized as wires in a circuit. To achieve a more precise determination of the freezing point of hydrogels, differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) analysis was conducted (Fig. 5b). The freezing temperatures of the hydrogels containing Ag-PDA@SiO2 and Ag-PDA@SiO2 with glycerol were found to be −12.7 °C and − 45.3 °C, respectively. These results indicate that the incorporation of glycerol significantly lowers the freezing temperature of the hydrogel.

To further investigate the effect of glycerol on the moisture retention properties of the hydrogels, cylindrical samples of both types (Group W and G) of hydrogels were maintained at a constant temperature (25 °C). The weight and morphological changes of the samples were recorded at 10-h intervals. As illustrated in Fig. 5c, following water evaporation, the group W hydrogels underwent desiccation and internalization after seven days, resulting in a significant loss of swellability and stretchability. In contrast, the group G hydrogels demonstrated a notable retention of their swellability and stretchability properties, which can be attributed to the moisturizing effect of the incorporated glycerol. An analysis of the weight changes of the hydrogels presented in Fig. 5d indicates that the group W hydrogels lost nearly 60 % of their initial weight after only 40 h of storage. In comparison, the group G hydrogels lost only 25 % of their initial weight during the same period. In addition, electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) results showed continued reliability of electrical conductivity of hydrogels in group G (Fig. 5e). The enhanced antifreezing and moisturizing properties of the glycerol-induced hydrogels could be ascribed to the formation of hydrogen bonds between the hydroxyl groups of water molecules and glycerol. This interaction lowers the freezing point of the water within the hydrogels, thereby preventing the formation of ice crystals and facilitating the retention of a substantial amount of water [51].

The integration of ionic liquid and Ag-PDA@SiO2 nanoparticles significantly enhances the electrical conductivity of the hydrogel, as demonstrated in Fig. 6a. When the PAM/CMC/PEG/Ag-PDA@SiO2 hydrogel sensor was connected to an LED light, the light bulb illuminated immediately upon circuit activation. Notably, the light bulb extinguished when the hydrogel was disconnected, but it illuminated again upon reconnection. To evaluate the potential of conductive hydrogels for wearable sensors, we investigated the electrical properties of the hydrogels. As illustrated in Figs. 6b and c, the hydrogel strain sensors exhibited excellent signal stability and repeatability under both small (10 %, 30 %, 50 %, 70 %, 90 %) and large (200 %, 500 %, 700 %) deformations. Furthermore, a clear correlation was established between the change in relative resistance of the sensors and the applied strain, indicating that higher strains resulted in more substantial resistance changes. The hydrogels were subjected to cyclic stretching and release between 0 % and 80 % strain at varying strain rates of 60, 90, 120, and 150 mm/min to assess their sensing performance. The results (Fig. 6d) demonstrated that the relative resistance of the hydrogel sensors changed more rapidly with increasing stretching rates. However, the resistance change remained independent of the stretching rate (Fig. 6e), indicating a stable conductive path.

The gage factor (GF), defined as the ratio of the relative resistance change to the applied strain, was utilized to evaluate the sensing sensitivity of the hydrogel sensors, as illustrated in Fig. 6f. The GF values were categorized into three stages based on strain: 0.37 for 0–50 %, 1.58 for 50–430 %, and 2.92 for 430–800 %. This categorization indicates that the sensitivity of the hydrogel increases with the applied strain. During the early stage of small strain stretching, the hydrogel matrix displayed heightened inhomogeneity alongside a decrease in its cross-sectional area. This change obstructs the flow of free ions, which results in a rise in resistance. Nevertheless, the ions contained within the hydrogel sustain consistent contact with each other, causing only a slight variation in resistance and, as a result, a lower GF. As tensile strain increases further, the spacing between ions increases, causing them to separate from each other. This separation restricts the ion movement path, resulting in a rapid increase in resistance and, thus, a higher GF.

As demonstrated in Fig. 6g, the response and recovery times of the hydrogel sensor at 2 % strain are 600 ms and 900 ms, respectively, indicating that the hydrogel sensor exhibits good responsiveness. Additionally, as depicted in Fig. 6h, the intensity of the relative change in resistance for the hydrogel sensor can exhibit considerable variation with changes in tensile length, all while remaining stable across different tensile strains. To further assess the sensors' durability and long-term stability, a series of 500 loading-unloading cycles were conducted within a strain range of 0–100 %. The findings presented in Fig. 6i reveal that the sensor's output signal did not show significant drift, and the relative change in resistance between cycles stayed largely consistent. The enlarged cyclic plots further illustrate the stability of the signal of the relative resistance change, a property that is favorable for utilization as a flexible sensor for long-term operation.

In this study, hydrogels are explored not only as sensors but also for their capability to encrypt information. It is well established that Morse code represents an early form of digital communication, where information is conveyed through various arrangements of dots and dashes (Fig. 7a) [52]. By utilizing Morse code, hydrogel sensors can effectively transmit encrypted information. As depicted in Fig. 7b, the hydrogel sensor is affixed directly to the finger to detect bending motions. The relative resistance changes corresponding to bending from 0° to 30° are represented by dots, while those corresponding to bending from 0° to 90° are depicted as dashed lines, arranged in distinct patterns to convey different messages. Fig. 7c illustrates the transmission of punctuation marks, letters of the alphabet, and the message ‘I love JLU’. The consistency and stability of the current signals validate the reliability of the hydrogel sensor, thus demonstrating its potential applications in information encryption and message transmission.

The information recorded with fluorescent substances is not visible under visible light. However, it can be easily observed under light irradiation of specific wavelengths [53]. Additionally, the hydrogel's excellent mechanical properties effectively protect the encoded information and resist physical damage, indicating that fluorescent hydrogels have significant potential in the field of information encryption materials. The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of the composite hydrogel at different excitation wavelengths were tested, revealing that the optimum excitation light wavelength is 340 nm, as shown in Fig. 8a. The observed shift in the spectral lines is attributed to the excitation-dependent nature of the carbon quantum dots. The fluorescence intensity of hydrogels doped with varying concentrations of carbon dots (CDs) under 340 nm excitation light is depicted in Fig. 8b, while the morphology of CDs is illustrated in Fig. 8c. To ascertain the protective efficacy of the hydrogel against fluorescent information, as depicted in Fig. 9d, it was observed that the hydrogel containing the fluorescent information demonstrated the ability to regain its original shape following deformation, with no discernible damage to the fluorescent information (Fig. 9e). A multiple encryption strategy was devised to enhance the level of anti-counterfeiting (Fig. 9f). This strategy utilized the fluorescent hydrogel to create a binary cipher, in which the letters A-Z correspond to the five-digit binary code ranging (00000−11,001). A fluorescent array was presented using the fluorescent hydrogel and the hydrogel without CDs, which did not reveal any information under sunlight. In contrast, the fluorescent pattern (representing ‘1’) and the non-fluorescent pattern (representing ‘0’) were clearly visible under UV light at 340 nm. The decryption process consists of three steps. First, the fluorescent array is observed under ultraviolet (UV) light. Second, the green fluorescent pattern and the non-fluorescent pattern are converted into the binary numbers ‘1’ and ‘0’, respectively. Finally, the letters ‘JLU’ are translated based on a specific binary code.

PAM/CMC/PEG/Ag-PDA@SiO2 hydrogel sensors have been shown to exhibit excellent mechanical and sensing properties, making them suitable for use as wearable sensors for detecting various human body motions. These sensors were directly attached to different parts of the human body, including the fingers, cervical vertebrae, wrist joints, and knee joints, without the need for adhesive tape, and their ability to sense physiological movements was evaluated. As illustrated in Fig. 9a, an increase in relative resistance was observed as the finger bending angle increased from 0° to 90°. Furthermore, when the finger bending was reversed, the resistance returned to its initial state, allowing the degree of finger bending to be recognized through the measurement of the change in relative resistance. Furthermore, the strain sensor based on hydrogel, which employs PAM/CMC/PEG/Ag-PDA@SiO2, showcased its capability to sense subtle body movements, including activities such as swallowing, throat motions, and chewing, as well as cervical joint movements (Fig. 9d, e, and f). The relative resistance changes observed were approximately 10 %, 5 %, and 13 %, respectively. In addition, these hydrogel-based sensors also excelled in detecting significant human movements, such as the flexion of wrists and knees (Fig. 9g and h), with relative resistance changes of 50 % and 28 %, respectively (Fig. 5c, d, and f). It is noteworthy that human motions (swallowing, chewing, neck movement, running) could be distinctly identified by the changes in signal. These results demonstrate the sensor's high accuracy and sensitivity, indicating its promising application in human motion monitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogel strain sensor based mood monitoring system for dogs
ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, more and more people are choosing to incorporate pets into their lives, and these furry companions have become an integral part of the family. Along with increased care for pets, people are also paying more attention to their emotional needs and health. Notably, the emotional state of canines, one of the most popular human companions, directly influences the quality of interactions with their owners and even their health. Canines primarily express their emotions through body language and behavioral actions, with tail movement being a crucial component of their emotional expression. The frequency, amplitude, and posture of tail wagging can serve as indicators of a dog's emotional state. In this context, the present study proposes a novel approach that integrates hydrogel flexible sensor technology with pet behavior monitoring, thereby developing a dog emotion monitoring system based on hydrogel strain sensors. This innovative solution aims to empower owners to better understand their dogs' emotional changes.

As demonstrated in Fig. 10a, the motion state of the dog's tail is detected in real-time by affixing a sensor to the tail. When the tail moves, the sensor experiences slight deformation, triggering a change in its relative resistance. The output electrical signals correspond to the various motion states of the dog's tail. To monitor the dog's mood fluctuations throughout the day, a mobile application (Fig. 10b) was developed. This application enables the phone to receive signals from the sensors in real-time via a wireless communication module (e.g., Bluetooth or Wi-Fi), converting these electrical signals into digitized time-series data. This data can be analyzed to identify and predict the dog's current mood, as well as the characteristics of tail motion, including speed, amplitude, and rhythm. The current emotional state of the dog is identified and recorded on the mobile device. The application's design emphasizes human-computer interaction, allowing users to visualize the dog's mood trends through a timeline graph. Furthermore, the application interface supports the observation of the emotional states of multiple dogs across different time periods and enables simultaneous monitoring of their emotional health. As shown in Fig. 10c-h, preliminary experiments indicate that the sensor can output characteristic and stable electrical signals under varying strains of the dog's tail. The hydrogel sensor was adhered to the upper end of the tail while the dog was ambulatory and relaxed, and the electrical signals from its tail movements were recorded throughout the day. These signals were summarized into six characteristic curves: relaxed, alert, aggressive, excited, submissive, and stressed.

While the system remains in the experimental stage, its potential applications are promising. This technology could assist pet owners in better understanding their pets' emotional needs and improving their interactions with them. Furthermore, it provides a novel tool for animal behavioral studies, enabling researchers to analyze the mechanisms of emotional expression in dogs more objectively. Future research will focus on enhancing the durability of sensors and signal processing algorithms, classifying measurements across various dog breeds, integrating data analysis algorithms within mobile software, and constructing individualized emotion models based on historical data. These objectives aim to improve the identification and prediction of different dog breeds and personality traits. Additionally, there are plans to extend the technology to other pet species, to advance its application across a broader range of fields.

This study successfully developed a multifunctional PAM/CMC/PEG/Ag-PDA@SiO2 hydrogel sensor. The hydrogel sensor exhibited an extensive mechanical strain range of 1432 %, a tensile strength of 0.38 MPa, a rapid response time of 600 ms, high sensitivity (GF = 2.92, within 430–800 %), and impressive fatigue resistance over 500 cycles. Additionally, the incorporation of Ag-PDA@SiO2 nanoparticles imparted effective antimicrobial properties to the hydrogel sensor. Glycerol enhances the hydrogel's frost resistance and moisture retention, while ensuring flexibility at −20 °C and preserving conductivity. These attributes highlight its potential for applications in extreme environments. Furthermore, carbon quantum dots enable the hydrogels to fluoresce under UV irradiation, thereby revealing topographical information. The hydrogel can also convey information through variations in relative electrical resistance, indicating its potential for information encryption applications. The strain sensor based on this hydrogel is capable of detecting various human motions and canine tail movements with high sensitivity, good stability, and robust characteristics. Collectively, these exceptional properties of the PAM/CMC/PEG/Ag-PDA@SiO2 hydrogel suggest promising applications for flexible sensors in information encryption, human motion monitoring, and animal behavioral studies.

Xu Ji: Writing – original draft. Zhang Yu: Software, Resources. Mengjiao Nan: Validation, Investigation. Rongfeng Ge: Methodology, Investigation. Yin Gao: Visualization, Validation. Hong Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Zhenchun Li: Writing – review & editing. Lei Wang: Writing – review & editing.

All experiments in this work were performed in accordance with the current laws of the countries/regions where they were performed. The strain data of the dog's tail was measured by a mechanical model. All the volunteers employed in the adhesion test and the human motion sensing test are the authors of this manuscript, and the gender of the volunteers had no impact on the research results. These tests did not require any approval from the ethics committee.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled “Cellulose nanocomposite hydrogel-conductive, antimicrobial and high tenacity sensor for information encryption, human motion monitoring and animal behavior studies”.
